text: TitleChlorophyll-derived dietary antioxidants inhibit iron-dependent and iron-independent mechanisms of oxidative stress Abstract Chlorophylls constitute a ubiquitous group of dietary antioxidants, the antioxidant activities of which have not yet been completely defined. We investigated chlorophylls a and b and their corresponding magnesium-free derivatives, pheophytins a and b, in relation to their capacities to protect against different forms of oxidative stress in vitro using complementary assay strategies. Our experiments involved (i) assessment of the capacity to counteract oxidative DNA strand breaks (SBs) formed in response to H2O2 using the comet assay; (ii) evaluation of the scavenging activities of the test compounds towards DPPH• using spectrophotometry; (iii) measurement of iron chelation using atomic absorption spectrometry; and (iv) assessment of the capacity to inhibit the formation of thiobarbituric acid reactive species (TBARS) during the copper-dependent peroxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL). The antioxidant activities of the four chlorophyll derivatives were dose-dependent and, relative to their potential to inhibit iron-dependent mechanisms of oxidative stress, pheophytins a and b demonstrated greater capacity than their corresponding chlorophyll derivatives, whereas the reverse was true for iron-independent oxidative stress mechanisms as assessed in relation to their ability to protect against H2O2-induced DNA damage. Thus, in relation to copper-dependent lipid peroxidation of LDL and iron-dependent mechanisms of oxidative DNA strand break formation, the tested chlorophyll derivatives, through their capacity to scavenge free radicals and chelate transition metals, could constitute a defensive barrier against iron-dependent and iron-independent oxidative stress pathways.
